title: At the town office
setting: You've come to the Gemeinde to register now that you've moved to Lucerne.

steps.speaker: Beamtin
steps.es: Grüezi. Wie chan ich Ihne hälfe?
steps.en: Hello. How can I help you?

options.es: Grüezi. Ich möcht mi aamälde, ich bi nöi zoge.
options.en: Hello. I'd like to register, I just moved here.
options.feedback: Clear and polite — exactly what she needs to start.
Understandable, just abrupt. A full sentence fits an office better.

options.es: Ich weiss nöd genau, wieso ich da bi.
options.en: I'm not sure why I'm here.
options.feedback: If unsure, just say you've moved and need to register.

steps.speaker: Beamtin
steps.es: Gärn. Händ Sie en Uswiis und de Mietvertrag dabii?
steps.en: Gladly. Do you have ID and the rental contract with you?

options.en: Yes, I have everything with me.

options.feedback: A smart question if unsure — better to ask than be turned away.
You can't register without documents. Always bring them.

steps.speaker: Beamtin
steps.es: Guet. Füled Sie das Formular us und ünnerschriibed da.
steps.en: Good. Fill out this form and sign here.

options.feedback: Asking to be sure on an official form is the right instinct.
Fine if you're sure — but confirming where never hurts.
Relatable! But it's required — just work through it.

steps.speaker: Beamtin
steps.es: Alls in Ornig. D'Bewilligung chunnt mit de Poscht.
steps.en: All in order. The permit will come by mail.

options.en: Thank you very much for your help.
options.feedback: Polite and warm — the right note to end on.
That's normal here. Complaining just sours things.
